fake_mutual_funds.description: Fraudsters create fake mutual fund schemes promising high returns with little or no risk. They use fake documents, websites, and agents to lure investors.
red_flags: Guaranteed high returns with low risk
Pressure to invest quickly
Unregistered agents or companies
Complex investment structures not easily understood
Requests for cash payments or unconventional payment methods
fake_mutual_funds.real_example: In 2020, SEBI busted a fake mutual fund scheme operating in Maharashtra that collected over ₹500 crores from investors by promising 30% monthly returns.
fake_mutual_funds.verification_step: Always verify the mutual fund's registration with SEBI at sebi.gov.in → Mutual Funds → List of Registered Mutual Funds

predatory_loan_apps.description: Illegal lending apps that offer instant loans with minimal documentation but charge exorbitant interest rates and use deceptive practices for recovery.
red_flags: Instant loan approval without proper verification
Interest rates much higher than bank rates (often 30-50% monthly)
Hidden charges and processing fees
Aggressive recovery tactics including harassment
Access to contacts, photos, and other personal data
predatory_loan_apps.real_example: In 2021, RBI warned about 60+ illegal lending apps operating in India that were trapping borrowers in debt cycles with interest rates exceeding 100% annually.
predatory_loan_apps.verification_step: Check if the lending app is registered with RBI as an NBFC at rbi.org.in → NBFC List

ponzi_schemes.description: Investment scams where returns are paid to earlier investors using funds from newer investors, creating an illusion of profitability until the scheme collapses.
red_flags: Returns that seem too good to be true (consistently high monthly returns)
Emphasis on recruiting others to earn more money
Lack of clear investment strategy or product
Complex commission structures
Pressure to reinvest earnings rather than withdraw
ponzi_schemes.real_example: The Saradha Group chit fund scam of 2013 collected over ₹20,000 crores from investors across Eastern India by promising high returns on chit fund investments.
ponzi_schemes.verification_step: Verify any investment scheme with SEBI or RBI before investing. Remember: if it sounds too good to be true, it probably is.

whatsapp_investment_tips.description: Fraudsters use WhatsApp groups to share 'insider tips' about stocks or investments that are guaranteed to make money, often pumping up penny stocks.
red_flags: Unsolicited investment tips via WhatsApp/Telegram
Claims of 'insider information' or 'secret tips'
Pressure to act fast before opportunity disappears
Focus on low-priced, volatile stocks (penny stocks)
Guarantees of profit or minimum returns
whatsapp_investment_tips.real_example: In 2022, SEBI issued warnings about multiple WhatsApp groups spreading false information about stocks to manipulate prices and defraud investors.
whatsapp_investment_tips.verification_step: Never act on unsolicited investment tips. Always do your own research and verify information from official sources like company websites or stock exchanges.

fake_govt_schemes.description: Scammers create fake government welfare schemes or modify existing ones to trick people into sharing personal information or paying money for non-existent benefits.
red_flags: Requests for payment to process government benefits
Asking for bank details or OTPs to verify eligibility
Schemes not listed on official government websites
Poor quality documents with spelling/grammar errors
Urgent calls to action with limited time offers
fake_govt_schemes.real_example: During the pandemic, fake PM Garib Kalyan Yojana schemes were circulated asking people to pay processing fees to receive benefits that never existed.
fake_govt_schemes.verification_step: Always verify government schemes on official websites ending in .gov.in or .nic.in. Never pay money to receive government benefits.

chit_fund_fraud.description: While legitimate chit funds exist, fraudsters run illegal chit fund schemes that promise high returns but disappear with investors' money.
red_flags: Unregistered chit fund companies
Returns significantly higher than bank FD rates
Lack of transparent auction process
Pressure to recruit more members
Difficulty in withdrawing funds or getting paperwork
chit_fund_fraud.real_example: In 2019, the Karnataka Police busted a chit fund scam that collected over ₹100 crores by promising 40% annual returns but operated without proper registration.
chit_fund_fraud.verification_step: Verify chit fund registration with your state government's chit fund registrar or the Registrar of Companies.

upi_scam.description: Fraudsters trick users into sharing UPI PINs or OTPs, or use fake UPI QR codes to steal money directly from bank accounts.
red_flags: Requests for UPI PIN or OTP (legitimate services never ask for these)
Fake UPI QR codes sent for 'receiving money'
Links requesting UPI app installation or verification
Calls pretending to be from bank asking for UPI details
Screenshots showing money received when actually sent
upi_scam.real_example: In 2021, NPCI reported over 50,000 UPI fraud cases monthly, with victims losing money through social engineering tactics rather than technical breaches.
upi_scam.verification_step: Never share your UPI PIN or OTP with anyone. Always verify UPI IDs and QR codes before scanning. Use official bank apps only.
